analysis_steps: Identified the dominant peak at 247.57 cm⁻¹ as the primary diagnostic feature with prominence >1000
Noted secondary strong peak at 518.43 cm⁻¹ and moderate peaks at 392 and 306 cm⁻¹ in the fingerprint region
Recognized the low-frequency peak at 132.60 cm⁻¹ as a possible lattice or rigid-layer mode
Compared the 248/392/518 cm⁻¹ peak pattern against known materials: MoS₂, elemental sulfur, TiO₂, and other TMDs
Noted extremely high peak density above 1500 cm⁻¹ with monotonically increasing intensity — attributed to contamination, fluorescence background, or noise artifacts rather than intrinsic Raman modes
Concluded MoS₂ (2H phase, bulk) as most likely candidate given the strong low-frequency mode structure, though calibration uncertainty reduces confidence
Classified sample purity as contaminated due to extensive high-frequency spectral features inconsistent with a pure inorganic material
decision_trace: Strongest peak at 247.57 cm⁻¹ with very high prominence (1068), dominating the spectrum
Second strongest Raman peak at 518.43 cm⁻¹ (prominence 536)
Additional peaks at 392.42 cm⁻¹ and 305.91 cm⁻¹ in the lattice mode region
Peak at 132.60 cm⁻¹ suggests low-frequency lattice or rigid-layer mode
Massive number of peaks above 1500 cm⁻¹ with increasing intensity suggests contamination, fluorescence, or noise artifacts
The ~248 and ~518 cm⁻¹ doublet with ~392 cm⁻¹ is broadly consistent with a transition metal dichalcogenide or sulfur compound
